7.1:
What beauty adorns your feet in sandals, O daughter of nobility!
The sculpted curves of your thighs are like fine-wrought ornaments,
fashioned by a master craftsman's hands.
7.2:
Your navel — a bowl of rounded grace, ever filled with mingled wine;
your belly like a mound of wheat, ringed round about with lilies.
7.3:
Your breasts are like a pair of young fawns —
twin offspring of a gazelle.
7.6: How beautiful! How exquisite you are, my love — pure delight embodied.
7.7:
You stand tall as a palm tree,
and your breasts like the full clusters of dates at its crown.
7.8:
I resolve to climb that palm tree and grasp its fruit-laden branches.
Let your breasts be like clusters of the vine,
and every breath you draw as sweet as apples.
7.9:
And your kisses — like the finest wine,
pouring smooth and sweet for my beloved,
gliding past the lips of those at rest.
7.10: I belong wholly to my beloved — and his longing is toward me alone.
7.11:
Come away, my love — let us go out into the open fields
and spend the night among the scattered villages.
7.12:
At dawn let us go to the vineyards —
to see whether the vines have budded, whether their blossoms have opened,
whether the pomegranates have come into flower.
There, in that garden-place, I will give you my love.

8.1: I wish you were like a brother to me — nursed at my own mother's breast! Then if I found you in the open street I could kiss you freely, and no one would think less of me.
8.2: I would take you by the hand and bring you into my mother's house — that place of learning and of love — and give you spiced wine to drink, the sweet-pressed juice of my own pomegranate.
8.3: His left hand cradles my head; his right arm wraps around me.
8.4:
I put you on oath, daughters of Jerusalem:
do not rouse love, do not stir it from its sleep —
not before it is ready, not before the time.
8.5:
Who is this rising from the desert, leaning on the arm of her beloved?
Beneath the apple tree I woke you —
on the very ground where your mother went into labor,
where she who bore you first felt her birth-pangs.
8.6:
Seal me upon your heart — seal me upon your arm —
for love is as relentless as death,
and jealous devotion as fierce as Sheol's own grip.
Its embers are fire's coals — a flame that is Yah's very breath,
the greatest of all flames.
8.7:
No rush of many waters can quench love; no flood can drown it.
Should a man offer his entire fortune for love,
he would only earn contempt.
8.8: We have a younger sister — still a child, with no breasts yet to speak of. What will we do for her on the day she is sought in marriage?
8.9: If she proves steadfast as a wall, we will crown her with silver battlements; if she opens freely like a door, we will board her in with cedar planks.
8.10: I am a wall, and my breasts are towers of strength — and in his eyes I am one who has found shalom.
8.11: Solomon kept a great vineyard at Baal-hamon, leased to tenant farmers — each one owing a thousand silver pieces for the fruit of its harvest.
8.12:
But my vineyard — mine alone — is entirely my own.
Keep your thousand pieces, Solomon;
let two hundred go to the keepers of the vines.
My love is not for sale.
8.13:
You who dwell among the gardens —
your companions strain to hear your voice.
Let me hear it.
8.14:
Come swiftly, my love!
Leap like a gazelle — like a young stag bounding
over mountains heavy with spice.

1.2:
Kiss me — on the lips, with your lips.
There is no wine as intoxicating as your love.
(The abrupt shift from 'let him' to 'your love' is in the Hebrew: the heart speaking aloud and then addressing him directly, unable to hold the third person.)
1.3: Your name itself is fragrance — not just the oils you wear, but the word that names you, released into the air like perfume uncorked. Of course they love you. How do you not love a man whose very name is oil poured out?
1.4:
Take me with you — I want to run where you run.
He led me into the innermost rooms.
And the joy that follows is undeniable: your love is better than any wine,
and everyone who loves you is right to do so.
1.5:
Do not let my darkness deceive you.
Kedar's tents are black and they are beautiful;
Solomon's curtains are dark and they are rich.
I am dark and I am lovely.
The sun has worked on me; it has not made me less.
1.6:
Stop looking at me that way — as though my darkness were a flaw.
The sun did this.
My brothers were angry and sent me out to work the family fields, and I went.
But while I kept their vines I could not keep myself —
could not tend the garden that is my own life, my own person.
1.7:
Tell me where you are —
the one my whole self reaches toward.
Where do you take your flock in the heat of the day?
I don't want to find you by accident,
wandering past the camps of other shepherds
like someone who doesn't know where she belongs.
1.8:
If you truly don't know — and perhaps you do know, and only wish to be invited —
then follow the flock's trail.
Graze your goats beside the other shepherds' camps.
You will find me there, or you will find the way there.
1.9:
You are like a mare running loose among Pharaoh's battle stallions —
beauty that undoes all discipline,
that causes chaos among the very instruments of power.
They are trained; you are free.
They are impressive; you are overwhelming.
1.10:
The jewelry frames what was already beautiful and makes it more so.
Your cheeks in their ornamented setting, your neck in its strings of beads —
each ornament is a setting for the face and throat themselves,
which need no ornament.
1.11:
Whatever adorns you now, we will make more —
gold for you, studded with silver,
worked for someone worth working for.
1.12:
He was there, reclining, waiting —
and my nard spoke before I could.
The perfume announced me, rose to him across the room.
There are conversations the body has before the person arrives.
1.13:
The way I carry him —
like a packet of myrrh worn against the heart,
giving off its fragrance all day and all night,
warming with body heat
until the whole self smells of him.
1.14:
He is that particular luxury —
not just henna flowers, but En-gedi henna,
the finest quality, growing in the oasis beside the salt sea.
An abundance of beauty in the middle of harsh terrain.
That is what he is to me.
1.15:
Let me look at you. Let me look again.
Dove eyes — soft, still, attentive,
seeing without the predator's sharpness.
Your beauty is not hard or aggressive;
it is the kind that asks to be looked at slowly.
1.16:
And back to you: beautiful and truly pleasant to be near.
Our bed is the green earth itself —
the forest floor is our bridal chamber,
the living world our canopy.
1.17:
Cedar beams, cypress rafters — the finest wood, the fragrant wood.
The whole outdoors is a house built for us,
aromatic and strong.

2.1:
I am not the exotic cultivated bloom —
I am the common flower of the open plain,
the lily that grows in low places.
Not rare, not imported, not hothouse-kept.
Wildflower beauty.
2.2:
Whatever she says about being a common wildflower —
she is the only one in the field that is not a thorn.
A lily in a briar patch.
Everything around her scratches; she does not.
2.3:
He is not like the other men —
they are tall and useless like forest trees,
giving shade but no fruit.
He gives fruit.
I rested under him and ate, and it was sweet.
There is both pleasure and nourishment in him,
which is rarer than either alone.
2.4:
The house of wine, and above everything —
above all the occasion, all the gathering, all the feast's abundance —
a single banner: love.
That is what announces us.
That is what flies over whatever space we enter together.
2.5:
I need to be held up.
This love has made me weak the way a fever makes you weak —
not sick in the ruinous sense, but overwhelmed, undone.
Bring me something sweet and solid;
I need to be restored to standing.
2.6:
This is the rest:
his left hand beneath my head, his right arm around me.
I am held from below and held from the side.
There is nowhere I am not supported.
2.7:
I am asking you —
by everything gentle and free in this world,
by the gazelles and the does that cannot be hurried or commanded —
do not rush this.
Do not try to force the moment that belongs to its own timing.
Love has a readiness; wait for it.
It will wake when it wills.
2.8:
I hear him before I see him.
Then — there — bounding over the ridge
like nothing between us is an obstacle.
Mountains are not barriers when he is coming toward me.
He leaps them.
2.9:
He has arrived but hasn't come in yet.
Like a deer at the edge of the clearing —
present but still wild,
still lit with the energy of having run here.
He looks through the lattice.
His eyes find me before he comes to the door.
2.10:
He called me out.
Those words — Arise. Come away.
As though staying inside were no longer possible,
as though the world outside were wide and he was in it,
and he wanted me there with him.
2.11:
'The hard season is finished.
There is no reason left to stay indoors.'
2.12:
'Everything is in bloom.
The birds are singing again.
The turtledove — which was silent all winter — has found its voice.
The whole world is telling you it is time to come out.'
2.13:
'The fig tree is already setting fruit.
The vines are in flower and the whole vineyard smells of it.
Everything is ready. Come out. Come away with me.'
2.14:
'You tuck yourself away like a dove in the rock-face —
visible only in outline, half-hidden.
Come out from that shelter.
I want to see you fully, hear you clearly.
Your voice is one of the best sounds in the world;
your face is one of the best sights.
Why are you hiding them from me?'
2.15:
'There are small things that can undo everything:
the little foxes that get into vines just as they're flowering,
nipping the blossoms before the fruit can form.
Catch them.
Our vineyard is at its most vulnerable and most beautiful moment —
it must not be spoiled by small, sneaking things.'
2.16:
Mine and his — the mutual belonging that is the core of what they have.
He is where the lilies are, in the sweet places,
and I know where to find him.
2.17:
Stay while the night remains.
When the day breathes its first breath and the shadows dissolve — only then go.
But until then: run these mountains the way you run everything,
leaping and free,
and come back to me.

3.1:
The dark is the time when absence becomes acute.
She lies in the place where he should be and he is not there.
She reached for him and found nothing.
The seeking begins.
3.2:
Decision: she will not wait for morning.
Up, into the city, through every street and square.
The whole city is smaller than her need to find him.
But the city is empty of him, or he is not where she looks.
3.3:
The watchmen on their rounds —
the men whose job is to see everything in the city at night —
she stops them and asks.
She is not embarrassed to ask, does not pretend to be out for another reason.
Have you seen him? That is all she needs to know.
3.4:
Almost immediately past them — suddenly, not gradually — he was there.
She held on.
She was not going to lose him a second time;
she brought him home, into the house of her beginning,
the room where she first became herself.
She wanted him in the place where she was most fully known.
3.5:
Again the refrain, after finding: still the same.
Even in the finding, even in the holding, even in the bringing-home —
the gift is not yours to force.
It woke when it willed. It will again.
3.6:
A procession out of the wilderness —
a plume of incense rising like a pillar,
carrying its fragrance ahead of itself:
myrrh, frankincense, every spice the merchants carry.
Something enormous and aromatic is coming up from the desert.
What is it?
3.7:
Solomon's palanquin — the bridal throne built for this day —
carried up from the desert,
and around it sixty of Israel's best fighting men.
The marriage of the king arrives with an escort of warriors.
3.8:
Armed men who know what they are doing — swords at the thigh, not ceremonial.
They are guarding something that must arrive safely.
Even the most beautiful procession is protected by readiness for violence.
The night carries its dangers, and the king's beloved must pass through it unharmed.
3.9:
He built it himself — or had it built according to his own design.
Lebanon cedar, the finest, the fragrant.
Even the vehicle that carries his love is made of the best material.
3.10:
Silver posts, gold back, purple cushion —
and the inside, the part that surrounds the one who sits in it,
woven with love by the young women of the city.
They made it with their hands and with their hearts.
The most intimate part of the most magnificent thing
is the part that was made with love.
3.11:
Come and see this:
the king in the crown his mother placed on his head.
She crowned him for this day —
not for battle, not for politics, but for marriage.
The gladness of his heart is the occasion.
A king who is glad is a different kind of king,
wearing a different kind of crown.

4.1:
Beautiful — and then again, beautiful: he cannot stop looking.
Dove eyes — the same soft, steady, undivided attention as before.
And her hair: a dark moving stream of it,
like the goats you can see from a distance
flowing down the mountain in the late afternoon light,
dark against pale stone.
4.2:
Every tooth in place — white, paired, complete.
Newly-washed white sheep moving in perfect formation,
each with her twin beside her, none missing a lamb.
The point is wholeness. Nothing is absent.
4.3:
Scarlet lips, a fine thread of color.
And the cheeks: pomegranate-halves, the inner flush of the fruit —
not just the skin but its interior warmth, seeded and alive.
The veil makes everything more present, not less.
4.4:
The tower of David is a military structure adorned with trophies of battle —
shields hung on its walls as proof of what it has overcome.
Her neck is like this: strong, held high,
decorated not with fragility but with the emblems of something formidable.
She is not merely decorative; she is imposing.
4.5:
Gentle, paired, unhurried, at ease —
grazing in the sweetest place.
The image is tenderness, not power.
After the tower's strength, softness.
4.6:
All night until the day comes —
he will be where the fragrance is, where she is.
The mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense are not geographical;
they are her.
4.7:
Everything. Not beautiful with reservations, not beautiful with a list of exceptions.
The whole of her, without a flaw.
This is the declaration that belongs to a particular kind of looking —
the looking that love enables.
4.8:
She is somewhere remote and wild —
the high peaks, the places where lions and leopards den.
He calls her down from the dangerous altitude.
Amana, Senir, Hermon — named mountains, real danger.
And yet he is not afraid of what surrounds her.
He is calling her toward him, not rescuing her from;
she is his bride, not his ward.
4.9:
One look. One bead of your necklace.
He is not holding his heart in reserve —
it is already hers, has been since the first glance.
'My sister, my bride': both the intimacy of family and the covenant of marriage.
You are both to him. You are everything.
4.10:
The superlative runs in both directions now:
her love is better than wine, her fragrance surpasses all spice.
In chapter 1 she said his love was better than wine;
now he says the same of hers.
The praise becomes mutual, an echo, a conversation of wonder.
4.11:
Honeycomb and nectar at the lips;
honey and milk beneath the tongue —
sweetness layered on sweetness,
the flavor of her words and her presence.
And behind all of it the cedar-and-mountain smell of Lebanon,
which is the smell of the highest and finest things.
4.12:
She is not available to everyone.
The garden has a lock; the spring has a seal.
This is not captivity — it is the guarding of something that belongs to one.
A locked garden is not a diminished garden; it is a private one.
Its beauty is reserved.
4.13:
The locked garden has an abundance inside it:
pomegranates, the best fruits, henna and nard.
What is enclosed is not empty or withholding — it is full.
The privacy makes the richness more intense, not less.
4.14:
The catalogue continues: every fragrant thing that can be named.
The garden that is her is a garden of incomparable richness.
Every spice the ancient world valued is present.
This is an inventory of wonder.
4.15:
And inside the locked garden: water.
Not a cistern, not a pool — a fountain, a spring of living water,
streams coming from the highest mountains.
She is the source as well as the garden.
4.16:
She calls the winds to release her fragrance into the world —
north wind to carry it far, south wind to warm it.
And she speaks of the garden as his: come to your garden, eat from it.
The locked garden was locked for him;
the key was always his.
What was guarded for one is freely given to that one.

5.1:
He came. The garden opened to him;
he gathered everything it had to offer — myrrh, honey, wine, milk.
And then, as though to the whole created world:
Come. Eat. Drink your fill.
Love is not diminished by sharing —
its abundance invites participation.
5.2:
Sleep on the surface, wakefulness underneath —
that is love's condition.
She is asleep but not entirely absent.
And then his voice: four names for her in a single breath —
sister, love, dove, perfect one.
He has been out in the night, his hair wet with dew.
He has been waiting. He is asking.
5.3:
The moment of hesitation she will never forgive herself for.
Two reasonable excuses — both true, both insufficient.
She has undressed; she has washed her feet;
getting up means undoing all of that.
The excuses are real but they are excuses.
She is about to miss him.
5.4:
His hand through the latch-hole — reaching in, trying the lock.
And at that gesture, something inside her moved.
Too late, she understood what she was about to lose.
5.5:
She rose at last.
Her hands were already scented —
the myrrh she had been wearing, the fragrance of anticipation,
dripping now from her fingers onto the bolt she was too slow to open.
Even her tardiness was fragrant.
5.6:
She opened the door. He was gone.
The same kind of absence as chapter 3, but worse —
this time she had heard him, almost reached him,
and the hesitation cost her.
Her soul failed — the word is from fainting,
from the body's collapse under the weight of what happened.
She ran after him in the dark and called
and he was not there.
5.7:
This time the watchmen are not helpful — they are violent.
She is a woman alone in the city at night, and they treat her as such.
They strike her, wound her, take her veil.
The seeking that was poetry in chapter 3 has become something darker:
she has been hurt in the search.
The vulnerability of love is real.
5.8:
The adjuration returns, but changed.
Before it was a warning not to rush love;
now it is a message to be delivered: tell him.
Tell him that I am lovesick,
that what I am suffering is love,
that whatever he is making of his absence
I am not cured of wanting him.
5.9:
The daughters press her: what makes him worth this?
Worth the night-search, worth the wounds, worth the begging?
The question is not hostile — it is an invitation to describe him.
Tell us what you see in him.
She does.
5.10:
Radiant — white with light — and ruddy with life.
Both together: the color of someone fully alive in two registers at once.
And distinguished: whatever ten thousand men you gather, he stands out.
This is not bias; she is reporting what is simply visible.
5.11:
Gold head — the crown of him — and black hair in waves.
Gold and black: the most striking contrast.
His head is treasure.
5.12:
Her eyes were doves; now his are doves too.
But his are also jewels — bathed in milk, which is to say lustrous and clean,
set beside a pool, which is to say perfectly placed.
The dove quality they share: the soft, attentive, undivided gaze.
5.13:
Fragrance again — his very face is a garden.
Spice-beds at the cheeks; lily-lips that release myrrh with every word.
She is describing not just his appearance
but his presence, the sensory experience of being near him.
5.14:
Gold arms set with gemstones; ivory body inlaid with blue lapis.
She is describing a living treasure —
not cold like metal and stone, but alive, warm, moving.
The preciousness of him is in his actual substance, not just his ornament.
5.15:
The whole of him: alabaster legs on gold sockets —
strong, white, set on something that will not shift.
And his overall impression: Lebanon itself,
which means height, grandeur, fragrance, permanence.
He looks the way Lebanon looks —
like something that has been there a long time
and will be there long after you are gone.
5.16:
The last word of the description,
and the word she saves for last is not 'husband' or 'king' but 'friend.'
Everything she has said — the gold, the cedar, the jewels, the lilies —
and at the end: this is my friend.
This is my beloved.
And they are the same man.

6.1:
The daughters of Jerusalem have heard the description.
They understand now — they see why she searches.
They are offering to help.
This is what an honest account of love produces:
it draws others in, makes them want to find what she has found.
6.2:
She knew all along. Or she knew at the moment of speaking.
He is where he always is:
in the garden, among the fragrant things, gathering what grows there.
She did not need to search the city.
She knew where he would be.
6.3:
The covenant declaration, returned to after the worst night.
No matter what it brought — the absence, the wounds, the searching —
this has not changed.
She is his and he is hers.
He is where the sweetest things grow, and she knows it.
6.4:
Tirzah the northern capital, Jerusalem the southern —
two cities of incomparable beauty, the poles of the whole land.
And between them, his beloved.
But then the shift: awesome as an army under banners.
Not just beautiful but formidable.
She does not merely attract; she overwhelms.
6.5:
He told her dove eyes couldn't wound; now they are too much to bear.
He asks her to look away.
This is the power she has over him:
a single glance undoes his composure.
And then, as though recovering,
he returns to the catalogue — the waterfall of dark hair down the mountain.
6.6:
The same praise as chapter 4:2 — returned to now.
When a man is overwhelmed enough to look away from someone's eyes,
he steadies himself by returning to what he knows:
the familiar beauty of her, beginning at the beginning again.
6.7:
And the pomegranate-cheeks again, as in 4:3.
Repeated not because he has run out of words
but because these images are the settled vocabulary of his love for her.
The veil remains — some things stay private
even in the most intimate description.
6.8:
Every woman of the court and the household —
queens, concubines, all the unmarried — catalogued and numbered.
The numbers are large.
Against them all, he is about to say something.
6.9:
Against all the many: one.
She is the only one —
not numerically an only child, necessarily,
but the singular one, the particular unrepeatable person who is her.
And even the queens and concubines,
who might have reason for rivalry, called her blessed.
You cannot look at her and not praise her.
6.10:
Dawn, moon, sun —
three progressions of light across the sky,
from the first hint to the full blaze of day.
And then again the army.
This is what the daughters and queens asked when they saw her.
Who is this who makes the whole sky relevant?
6.11:
A moment of ordinary searching —
she went to the orchard, checking whether the season had arrived,
whether things were budding and blooming.
And then something happened that she didn't anticipate.
6.12:
Before she knew what was happening, desire moved her —
carried her somewhere she hadn't meant to go,
into the royal chariot, beside the one who wields both speed and power.
She didn't plan it; love moved her past the moment of decision.
(6:12 is the most difficult verse in the book; the MT reads 'my soul/desire set me among the chariots of my noble kinsman/a prince' — the T rendering holds the ambiguity.)
6.13:
The voices call her back —
she has gone somewhere, perhaps fled from the attention,
perhaps from the overwhelming moment of the chariot.
Come back. Let us see you.
And she — or he — answers: what would you see?
The Shulamite as she dances between two camps —
two worlds, the one she came from and the one she has entered.
('Shulamite' — שׁוּלַמִּית — appears only here; may echo shalom, wholeness.
'Two camps' — מַחֲנַיִם, Mahanaim — recalls Jacob's place of meeting at Gen 32.
She stands at the intersection of two realities. That is what you are looking at.)
